the interview. Uh, we I just want to
provide some broader context for what's
taking place here. So, the select
board's taking an important step forward
in the replacement and upgrade of the
outdoor ice skating rink at Lars
Anderson Park and the supporting
infrastructure around it. This project
also includes the stabilization and
historic restoration of the original
Anderson era elements within the project
area which are an important part of the
park's history and character. This is an
exciting and long-awaited project for
the town. It reflects years of work by
the park and recreation commission, town
staff, community members, and many
others who have helped evaluate the
future of the rink. Uh, by way of brief
background, in 2020, after the
refrigeration system at the Lars
Anderson Park ice rink failed, the Park
and Recreation Commission established an
ice rink task force to evaluate future
options for the facility. A preliminary
feasibility study was conducted in 2022
and the ice rink programming study was
completed in 2024.
Based on those findings, the park and
recreation commission voted to advance a
single open air rink and the select
board supported locating the replacement
rink at the existing site. In May 2025,
town meeting approved community
preservation act funding to advance
design work, public engagement, and the
preparation of construction documents
for the rink on the existing site.
This project is unique because it brings
together recreation, public buildings,
landscape, historic preservation, park
planning, and community use. For that
reason, the design review committee has
been structured to reflect a broad and
collaborative process. The design review
committee will include representatives
from the building commission and the
park and recreation commission who will
serve as chairs along with the select
board representative and uh members of
the preservation commission and planning
board. It will also include key
stakeholders including friends of Lars
Anderson Park youth hockey and two
members of the public and we just heard
two members of the public interview for
those spots. The role of the design
review committee will be to guide the
concept design phase, and help ensure
that the project meets community needs,
respects the site's historic character,
and aligns with the town's broader
goals. This phase will also allow the
town to refine project costs and better
understand potential construction
funding and grant opportunities. The
design review committee will serve in an
advisory role. It will make concept
design recommendations to the building
commission, park and recreation
commission, preservation commission, and
planning board. The design will
ultimately be reviewed and approved by
the select board. Once the concept
design is approved, the consultant team
will proceed with detailed design,
preparation of construction documents,
bidding, and necessary permitting. There
will be several public meetings as a
part of this process. And whether you
are a member of this committee or not,
all are of course welcome to attend and
opine.
Uh these meetings are scheduled for May
18th,
June 22nd, and July 30th. Additional
public meetings will be announced as
needed. These meetings will give
residents, user groups, neighbors, and
other stakeholders opportunities to
learn about the project, provide
feedback, and help shape the design as
it develops. The goal is to move through
a thoughtful, transparent concept design
process that produces a clear, buildable
plan, one that modernizes the rink,
respects the historic Anderson elements,
and improves the surrounding landscape
and infrastructure.

particular uh risks. Um the first risk

exempt from pl paying property taxes to
the town. Uh the 95-year tax certainty
agreement says that if a building is
sold to a taxexempt organization, then
the owner must continue to make payments
to the town equal to what the property

would be tied to the assessed value of
the building itself.

Brookline Select Board expressing its
appreciation to Kenneth Ken Liss for his
exemplary service and scholarship in
preserving and sharing the history of
Brooklyn. Whereas the Brooklyn Select
Board rightfully honors those exemplary
Brooklyn residents whose civic work,
scholarship, and leadership have
enriched the cultural and civic life of
our town. And whereas Kenneth Kin Liss
has served for many years as president
of the Brooklyn Historical Society,
leading the organization in its mission
to preserve, interpret, and promote
Brooklyn's heritage. And whereas under
the leadership of Ken List, the
historical society has maintained and
interpreted Brookline's historic
properties, including the Edward
Devotion House, one of the town's Otis
colonial era structures and headquarters
of the society, as well as the Widow
Harris House and the Putterham School at
Lars Anderson Park, thereby preserving
tangible links to Brookline's colonial
and early history. And whereas Kin Liss
has developed and led walking tours and
virtual tours of Brooklyn neighborhoods
and commercial corridors such as a
century and a half of shopping in
Koolage Corner, bringing to life the
stories of the people and places that
have shaped our community.
And whereas Kin List has overseen the
historical society's expansion of online
resources, making Brookline's history
widely accessible through digital
archives, photo collections,
neighborhood histories, and innovated
then and now features. Whereas Ken List
has contributed important research on
topics of civic and social significance
including the study of Brooklyn's
racially restrictive covenants published
as Thomas Aspenwald Davis, Amos Lawrence
and Brooklyn racial covenants which have
helped the town and its residents
confront difficult aspects of our past
with honesty and rigor. And whereas Ken
List has worked with other local public
historians and educators to provide
historical details and documentation for
their projects such as Hidden Brookline,
which provides walking tours and
uncovers and educates the public about
the history of enslaved people in the
town and the efforts of town residents
to undermine and abolish slavery. And
whereas Kim Lists has served as an
important and vital public historian,
giving lectures, writing extensively for
the historical society's website, and
contributing articles and commentary to
Brookline News and other local outlets
where his Brookline past and present
features have illuminated the historical
context of contemporary issues. And
whereas Kin List has worked in
partnership with town officials,
schools, civic groups, and cultural
institutions such as the public library
of Brooklyn using history to strengthen
community connections
and inform public discourse. And whereas
through these many many contributions,
Kin List has helped generations of
residents and students engage more fully
with Brooklyn's unique past, deepened
civic understanding of the town's
development, and inspired appreciation
for the role of history in shaping our
collective identity. Now therefore in

PROPOSITION TWO AND A HALF IS A
LAW PASSED BY VOTERS IN 1980
THAT LIMITS HOW MUCH CITIES AND
TOWNS CAN RAISE PROPERTY TAXES.
THE LAW HAS TWO LIMITS WITHIN
IT THE LEVY CEILING AND THE
LEVY LIMIT.
THE LEVY LIMIT DETERMINES HOW
MUCH A CITY MUNICIPALITY CAN
INCREASE PROPERTY TAX
COLLECTION FOR ONE YEAR TO THE
NEXT.
IT'S DETERMINED BY TAXES
GENERATED FROM NEW GROWTH SUCH
AS NEW RESIDENTIAL COMMERCIAL
DEVELOPMENT OR RENOVATIONS TO
EXISTING PROPERTIES THAT
INCREASE ASSESSED VALUE.
THE LEVY CEILING IS THE
ABSOLUTE MAXIMUM AMOUNT
OF PROPERTY TAXES THAT CAN BE
COLLECTED BY A CITY
MUNICIPALITY, A CITY A TOWN
CAN'T COLLECT MORE THAN 22. 5%
OF THE TOTAL VALUE OF ALL
TAXABLE PROPERTY WITHIN ITS
BORDERS.

MUNICIPALITIES RELY HEAVILY ON
